original_abstract:
This review examines the current state of chemical modeling in cold dense clouds (dark clouds), focusing on the critical intersection of kinetic databases, reaction networks, and astronomical observations. These regions, characterized by temperatures near 10 K and high visual extinction, serve as premier natural laboratories for testing our understanding of non-equilibrium interstellar chemistry. While traditional models emphasize gas-phase ion-neutral pathways initiated by cosmic-ray ionization, the discovery of complex organic molecules (COMs) and anions in quiescent environments has necessitated a more nuanced integration of grain-surface processes and non-thermal desorption mechanisms.

We provide a detailed evaluation of leading gas-phase chemical networks, such as KIDA and UMIST, by comparing their performance against observed molecular abundances in the benchmark sources TMC-1 and L134N. The analysis highlights significant successes, such as the prediction of CO, HCO$^+$, and various cyanopolyynes, but also identifies persistent discrepancies. Notably, gas-phase chemistry fails to account for observed methanol abundances, underscoring the necessity of surface-mediated synthesis. Furthermore, the "early-time" agreement favored by pseudo-time-dependent models is discussed in the context of cloud evolution, initial elemental abundances (C/O ratios), and the potential impact of physical dynamics on chemical clocks. By synthesizing recent laboratory measurements and theoretical calculations, this paper outlines the remaining uncertainties in reaction rates—particularly for neutral-radical and dissociative recombination processes—and identifies future priorities for improving the predictive power of astrochemical simulations in the era of high-sensitivity submillimeter astronomy.
humanized_abstract:
Chemical modelling of dark clouds—Status and perspectives Cold dense dark clouds are amongst the most studied interstellar media in the framework of astrophysical chemistry. This review is dedicated to the actual state of the art of the chemical modelling (as it is linked to kinetic databases, reaction networks and observations) of such objects. These objects consisting of gas at around 10 K and with high visual extinctions are unique natural environments to study a priori non-equilibrium chemistry in the most constrained conditions. Although classical models of dark clouds chemical composition involve gas phase ion-neutral reactions starting from cosmic rays ionization, the detection of complex organic molecules as well as of ions in quiescent sources impose a more sophisticated modelling including grain surface processes and non-thermal desorption.

The KIDA and UMIST gas-phase chemical networks have been investigated in relation to current modelling of cold dark clouds. We compare predictions of these networks with observational constraints for several abundant species which have been measured with high accuracy in the two benchmark sources, TMC-1 and L134N. The predictions for several species are very good, e.g. for CO, HCO$^+$ and cyanopolyynes. However, there are many poor predictions: one example is clearly methanol, which must have been formed on surfaces. We discuss why pseudo-time-dependent models rely heavily on a small "early time" period in the evolution of a cloud in order to fit observations as well as possible, and what this implies concerning the C/O ratios and initial physical state of clouds and their constituent chemical clocks, together with the role of transport processes in a cloud at different times. Using results of recent laboratory and theoretical studies of reaction rates, including neutral reactions and dissociative recombination processes, some of the outstanding and required astrochemical modelling advances for the high precision (i.e. low count rates) telescopes which will become available with the advent of next generation sub-millimetre astronomy are also discussed.

document.input:
This review examines the current state of chemical modeling in cold dense clouds (dark clouds), focusing on the critical intersection of kinetic databases, reaction networks, and astronomical observations.
